section.title: The Resurrection of Christ
section.sectionRationale: Paul opens the great resurrection chapter by returning to the foundation of everything: the gospel he preached, which the Corinthians received and in which they stand. He recites what he calls of first importance, an early confession that Christ died for our sins, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to many witnesses. This is the ground on which the whole argument of chapter fifteen will stand or fall, and it is the ground on which the Corinthians themselves are saved.
section.thesis: The gospel of first importance is that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, was buried, was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and appeared to many named witnesses, Paul last of all by sheer grace, and this good news, received and held firmly, is what saves.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the gospel received and held firmly; the confession of first importance, that Christ died, was buried, was raised, and appeared; and the witnesses to the risen Christ, with Paul last of all, an apostle only by grace.

units.label: The gospel you received and hold
units.note: the ground on which you stand and are saved

verses.text: Now, brothers, I want to remind you of the {{euangelion|gospel}} I preached to you, which you received, and in which you stand firm.
By this gospel you are saved, if you hold firmly to the word I preached to you. Otherwise, you have believed in vain.
For what I received I passed on to you as of first importance: that Christ died for our sins according to the {{graphe|Scriptures}},
that He was buried, that He was {{egeiro|raised}} on the third day according to the Scriptures,
and that He appeared to Cephas and then to the Twelve.

units.label: The witnesses, and Paul last of all

verses.text: After that, He appeared to more than five hundred brothers at once, most of whom are still living, though some have fallen asleep.
Then He appeared to James, then to all the apostles.
And last of all He appeared to me also, as to one of untimely birth.
For I am the least of the apostles and am unworthy to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God.
But by the {{charis|grace}} of God I am what I am, and His grace to me was not in vain. No, I worked harder than all of them, yet not I, but the grace of God that was with me.
Whether, then, it was I or they, this is what we preach, and this is what you believed.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, gospel. Not a set of tips or techniques but an announcement of what God has done in Christ. Paul reminds the Corinthians that the gospel is not one message among many but the message they received, in which they stand, and by which they are being saved. Its content is fixed: Christ crucified, buried, risen, and seen.

graphe.gloss: Writing, Scripture. Twice Paul says the death and resurrection of Christ happened according to the Scriptures, meaning the whole Old Testament pattern of a suffering and vindicated servant. The gospel is not a break with what came before but the fulfillment God had promised all along.

egeiro.gloss: To raise, to wake, to lift up. Used throughout the chapter for the resurrection. The passive form, He was raised, points to the Father raising the Son, a public act of God vindicating the crucified Christ. This is the hinge of the whole confession and of the whole chapter.

charis.gloss: Grace, unmerited favor. Paul, who persecuted the church, calls himself the least of the apostles, yet by grace he is what he is. Grace both makes him an apostle and empowers his labor, so that even his hard work is finally the grace of God at work in him. All boasting is excluded.

basic.context: Paul is beginning the last great section of the letter, on the resurrection. Some in Corinth were denying that the dead will rise. So Paul goes back to the very foundation: the gospel he first preached to them. He reminds them of an early Christian confession that he himself received and handed on, that Christ died for our sins, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to many people who were still alive to be questioned. This is the bedrock of the faith.
basic.hermeneutics: These verses preserve one of the oldest summaries of the gospel in the New Testament, older than the letter itself, which Paul received and passed on. He treats it as fixed and non-negotiable, of first importance. The repeated phrase according to the Scriptures shows the events were the fulfillment of God's long promise, not an accident of history. The list of eyewitnesses grounds the claim in public, checkable testimony.
basic.originalLanguages: The euangelion, the gospel, is an announcement of what God has done, not advice about what we must do. Paul says it all happened according to the graphe, the Scriptures, so the cross and empty tomb fulfill the Old Testament. The key verb is egeiro, He was raised, a passive pointing to God the Father raising the Son. And Paul explains his own apostleship by one word, charis, grace: the persecutor is now a preacher only because God was gracious to him.
basic.typology: Christ raised on the third day fulfills the pattern of vindication after suffering that runs through the Scriptures, from Jonah in the deep to the servant of Isaiah 53 who is cut off yet sees light. As the one who died for our sins and was raised, He is the true and final sacrifice and the firstfruits of a new humanity, a theme Paul will unfold in the verses that follow.
basic.christ: Everything here is about Christ: He died for our sins, He was buried, He was raised, and He appeared. The gospel is not first a set of ideas but a series of things that happened to Jesus. His death deals with our sins; His resurrection is God's public verdict that the work was accepted. The risen Christ then appears, again and again, to real people, and last of all to Paul himself.
basic.god: It is God who stands behind the whole confession. The Scriptures are His promise; the raising of Christ is His act; and the transformation of Paul the persecutor into Paul the apostle is His grace. God saves sinners through the gospel and calls even His enemies into His service, so that no one can boast except in Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 speaks of the servant who dies for the sins of many and is then vindicated. Luke 24 shows the risen Christ opening the Scriptures to explain His suffering and rising. Romans 4 says He was raised for our justification. Galatians 1 tells how the risen Christ appeared to Paul and made the persecutor a preacher.
basic.oneStory: This confession is the center of the whole Bible in a few lines. The promises of the Old Testament come to their point in a Christ who dies for sins and rises on the third day. From here the story runs outward to the whole world through the witnesses who saw Him, including a former enemy remade by grace, and forward to the resurrection of all who belong to Him.

context.literary: Chapter fifteen is the theological climax of the letter, and verses 1 to 11 are its foundation stone. Paul frames the section with the language of tradition, what I received I passed on to you, and quotes a compact confession in balanced clauses: died, buried, raised, appeared. He then extends the list of resurrection appearances and closes with his own case, moving from settled creed to personal testimony.
context.historical: Some Corinthians, shaped by Greek assumptions that the body is a prison and only the soul matters, were denying a future bodily resurrection. Paul answers not with philosophy but with history and testimony: a confession already circulating in the churches, and a roll of named eyewitnesses, more than five hundred at once, most still living, whom his readers could in principle question.
context.canonical: This is the earliest datable summary of the gospel in the New Testament, a creed Paul received within a few years of the events. It stands with the sermons in Acts and the Gospel passion and resurrection narratives as the church's foundational testimony, and it anchors the doctrine of resurrection that the rest of the chapter will defend and explain.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To recall the Corinthians to the fixed, received gospel as the foundation of their faith, to establish the death and resurrection of Christ as historical events attested by many witnesses and promised in Scripture, and so to lay the groundwork for defending the resurrection of believers.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 3 to 5 are confession, the settled facts of the gospel, not a command. Verse 2, if you hold firmly, and verse 1, in which you stand, carry the abiding call: to receive this gospel and keep holding it. The events are given; the fitting response is faith that endures.

debates.question: What does Paul mean when he says Christ died and rose according to the Scriptures?

views.label: Specific texts fulfilled
views.case: Paul has particular passages in view, such as Isaiah 53 for the atoning death and texts like Psalm 16 and Hosea 6:2 for rising, read as promising the third-day vindication.

views.label: The whole scriptural pattern
views.case: The phrase points more broadly to the entire Old Testament shape of a suffering, dying, and vindicated one, which the cross and resurrection bring to fulfillment, rather than to a single proof text.

debates.bearing: Either way the point holds: the gospel is the fulfillment of God's promise, not a novelty, and the Scriptures are its authoritative witness.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on a few weighty words. Euangelion, gospel, names an announcement of what God has accomplished in Christ, the message the Corinthians received and in which they stand. Twice Paul says these things happened according to the graphe, the Scriptures, binding the cross and the empty tomb to God's long promise. The pivotal verb is egeiro, He was raised, a divine passive: the Father raised the Son, publicly vindicating the crucified one. And Paul sums up his entire apostleship in charis, grace, for the persecutor of the church is now its preacher only because God was gracious, so that even his tireless labor is the grace of God at work in him.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the Scriptural pattern of vindication after suffering, from Jonah's three days in the deep, which Jesus named as a sign, to the servant of Isaiah 53 who is cut off yet prolongs his days.
Fulfills the sacrificial system and the sin-bearing servant of Isaiah 53, the true and final offering that takes away sin once for all.

study.christ: The gospel here is entirely Christ-shaped. He died for our sins, taking the place and penalty that were ours; He was buried, truly dead; He was raised on the third day, vindicated by God; and He appeared, bodily and repeatedly, to Cephas, the Twelve, five hundred at once, James, all the apostles, and last of all to Paul. The resurrection is not a symbol of springtime or a survival of the soul but the raising of the crucified Jesus, God's verdict that His atoning death was accepted. On this Christ everything in the chapter, and in the Christian hope, depends.
study.god: God is the unseen actor in every line. He gave the Scriptures that promised these things; He raised Christ from the dead; He saves those who believe the gospel; and He poured out grace on Paul, turning a violent enemy of the church into its hardest-working apostle. The transformation of Paul is itself a small gospel: God justifies the ungodly and calls them into His service, so that all boasting is in Him alone.

crossReferences.note: The servant who dies for the sins of many and is afterward vindicated and satisfied, the backdrop to Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures.
The risen Christ opens the Scriptures to show that the Messiah must suffer and rise on the third day, exactly Paul's according to the Scriptures.
Christ was delivered over for our trespasses and raised for our justification, linking His death and resurrection to our standing before God.
Paul receives the gospel by revelation and is called by grace, the persecutor made an apostle, matching verses 8 to 10.
The risen Christ appears to Saul on the Damascus road, the last of the resurrection appearances Paul recounts here.

study.oneStory: These few lines are the hinge of the whole Bible. The Scriptures had long promised a Christ who would suffer for sins and be vindicated, and here that promise lands in history: Jesus died, was buried, was raised, and was seen. From this center the story runs out to the nations through eyewitnesses, one of them a former enemy remade by grace, and it runs forward to the day when all who belong to Christ will share His resurrection. The gospel received is the gospel that saves.

quiz.q: What does Paul call of first importance?

options.text: That Christ died for our sins, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared.
options.note: This is the confession Paul received and passed on as the heart of the gospel.

options.text: That believers should stand firm and not quarrel.
options.note: Standing firm matters, but the first importance is the gospel facts themselves.

options.text: That the Corinthians should give to the collection.
options.note: That comes later in chapter sixteen, not here.

options.text: That spiritual gifts must be used in order.
options.note: That was chapters twelve to fourteen; here the focus is the gospel.

quiz.q: Why does Paul list so many eyewitnesses of the risen Christ?

options.text: To ground the resurrection in public, checkable testimony, including hundreds still living.
options.note: The appearances make the resurrection a matter of history, not private feeling.

options.text: To show that Jesus favored some disciples over others.
options.note: The list is about testimony, not favoritism.

options.text: To show the appearances were to many people, not to Paul alone.
options.note: True and important, though Paul's deeper aim is public, checkable testimony that grounds the resurrection in history.

options.text: To suggest the resurrection was only a vision in their minds.
options.note: Paul presents real appearances to real people, the opposite of a mere vision.

quiz.q: What does the phrase according to the Scriptures tell us about the gospel?

options.text: The death and resurrection of Christ fulfill God's promises in the Old Testament.
options.note: The gospel is the fulfillment of Scripture, not a novelty.

options.text: The gospel replaces and cancels the Old Testament.
options.note: Paul says it fulfills the Scriptures, not that it cancels them.

options.text: Paul was quoting a single verse only.
options.note: He may have texts in view, but the phrase points to the whole scriptural pattern.

options.text: The events had no connection to earlier Scripture.
options.note: The whole point is that they happened according to the Scriptures.

quiz.q: How does Paul explain his becoming an apostle?

options.text: By the grace of God, though he had persecuted the church.
options.note: Grace made the persecutor an apostle and empowered his labor.

options.text: By his hard work, which earned him the role.
options.note: He did work hard, but he insists it was not he but the grace of God with him.

options.text: By his natural gifts and training.
options.note: Paul credits grace, not his own qualifications.

options.text: By being the most worthy of the apostles.
options.note: He calls himself the least, unworthy to be called an apostle.

quiz.q: What does Paul say saves the Corinthians?

options.text: The gospel they received, if they hold firmly to it.
options.note: By this gospel you are saved, if you hold firmly to the word.

options.text: Their loyalty to Paul as their teacher.
options.note: Paul points them to the gospel, not to himself.

options.text: Their initial belief, whether or not they continue in it.
options.note: Close: they did believe, but Paul stresses holding firmly, otherwise you believed in vain.

options.text: Nothing can be known for sure about salvation.
options.note: Paul speaks with confidence about the saving gospel.

questions: Paul says the Corinthians are saved by holding firmly to the gospel. What does it look like for you to keep holding on to the gospel rather than drifting from it?
The gospel is first of all news about what Christ has done, not advice about what you must do. How does that reorder the way you think about your faith?
The resurrection rests on named eyewitnesses, many still living. How does the historical, public nature of the resurrection strengthen your confidence?
Paul, once a persecutor, was remade by grace into an apostle. Where do you need to trust that God's grace can redeem your past and use your life?
Paul worked harder than all, yet said it was not he but the grace of God. How can you labor wholeheartedly while giving God all the credit?

translationNotes.note: The phrase according to the Scriptures appears twice, once for the death for our sins and once for the raising on the third day, framing both events as the fulfillment of God's promise in the Old Testament.
He was raised renders a Greek passive, often called the divine passive, pointing to God the Father as the one who raised the Son, a public act of vindication.
One of untimely birth translates a vivid word for an abnormal or violent birth, Paul's humble image for his sudden, unexpected calling as the last to see the risen Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 15:1-11. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
